speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three, October 22, 1996. The survivor is Leo Zapiler being interviewed by Toni Binstock in Denver, Colorado, United States of America. The interview is being conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we start to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me-- when we ended the last tape, you were telling me about going to the camp for displaced persons. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About-- you went to Cham. Tell me about Cham. What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cham was a German town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German camp? No. It wasn't Germany. It was Czechoslovakia. It was Czechoslovakia. And Cham was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a camp. And we went to that camp. In that camp I started the electrician over the whole camp, taking care of the electrician. That why I had the right to handle something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I start sell beer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I sold a lot of beer. Two trucks a week. [LAUGHS] And I took care of the shower room, and the water, and electric. I took care of all the camp. That's why I had to sell beer. I sold beer, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two trucks a week. [LAUGHS] I was drinking so much beer. Until we decided to go from there to Germany. This was still Poland, I guess. I guess it was Poland. I don't remember. We decide to go to Germany. So I went to Limburg, looked in Limburg and I didn't see I can in Limburg do something. So I will decide to go further. And we went to-- where was I? I think you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where was I? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stuttgart. We went to Stuttgart. Stuttgart is a bigger town and we could do something there. When I came to Stuttgart I bought a car and I was taxi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a camp too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was private. I was private. I bought a car from a guy who was at the circus. And they gave me the license because Sunday you couldn't drive no car in Germany at this time. And they gave me the license for the circus. [LAUGHS] And I drove the car day and night. And I had gasoline, get it from the circus. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until I went to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed there in Stuttgart until what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1946 to '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family still together at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the kids were getting older.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was going to school. And I made a living with that car, a good living. I was driving all Germany, all over as a taxi. There was a lot of people. The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Americans didn't get dollars. They get scripts, they're called. [LAUGHS] And this script was a handle. They bought scripts and sold. [LAUGHS] So I was going with guys when they bought these scripts from the German soldiers. He sold it again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was driving around until I left to the States. Until 1950. I came to Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody help you to come to this country? Did the Joint or the UNRRA?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had relatives here, in Chicago. And I was thinking I go to the relatives, but the relatives didn't help me much. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they sponsor you to come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- I was registered to go to America. When my number came, they called me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have to wait?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wait about four months. Not too long. I didn't expect it so soon. It was about four months they called me and they gave me the papers to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, who were all the people who came with you in the family? Was your sister-in-law still with you, and your brother-in-law? Did they come with you too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They went to Israel. They came from Israel here a few years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who all in your family came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who all was that? Your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife and then-- Manya was already here. Manya had a different number.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she didn't come with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family. Me and my son. My wife, my son. Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you went to Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Chicago. I had cousins. And in Chicago I start working in a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: We make pumps. I worked half a year there. And then I worked in General Felt. They make carpets. And then I worked for myself. I bought a gasoline station. And I had the gasoline station until I left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till you left where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I left Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1950, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. 1950 I think you came.

speaker: interviewer
text: '56. And then where did you go then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I going? I think I going right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Denver, Colorado?

speaker: interviewee
text: Denver, Colorado.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this house. We bought this house. My wife came here visiting-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Manya was living here already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter, Manya.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came visit Manya and she bought this house. $42,000. Paid cash. And I came and just painted all. And I didn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you then-- you had a gas station in Chicago and you sold that then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had a gas station in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you moved here in 1956. Did you work after 1956? Or did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: You retired then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked-- well, there was my place, transmission place. I made a transmission in Chicago-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in Denver.

speaker: interviewee
text: On Colfax.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a transmission place in Denver?

speaker: interviewee
text: Transmission place. We bought a transmission place. Then we went in the [INAUDIBLE] business [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all together. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Until now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife was, with me, house wife. She didn't do anything. Never work. And she had sick and died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that, Mr. Zapiler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she died-- when your mother died?

speaker: interviewer
text: She died here in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you told me on your paper it was 1982.

speaker: interviewee
text: She died-- she already for 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: December 16th of '83.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I retired? Since I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you worked a long time. Do you still go to the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked, yeah. General Felt and the carpets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- do you still belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: BMH.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you still observe the Jewish religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your experiences-- leaving Poland and being in the prisoner of war camp-- did that have any long-lasting effect on you? Has it bothered you over the years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Was going through the life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Your life was like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say, Mr. Zapiler, to people who say it didn't happen? What would you say to those people who say it didn't happen? That the Holocaust didn't happen, that the war didn't happen. What would you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What would [INAUDIBLE]. The war-- I didn't suffer much from the war. Was I supposed to go to the army, I didn't go. I ran away. So I didn't-- had nothing with the war to do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was always on my side, not the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything I forgot to ask you about, or anything you want to tell about? About your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life is now waiting until I die. [LAUGHS] I'm secure it. I've got to want to live. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got enough. Got my house, my car, my yard, and I had a woman what I was supposed to marry, to live with her. She died a couple weeks ago. Now I'm by myself again. I loved her very much, but she got sick and died. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe I find another one, I don't know. Meantime, I'm not looking for.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any message that you want to leave for your kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to leave. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so very much for taking part.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I am dying, the kids taking over everything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't go over them nothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's theirs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for giving us your testimony today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. I thank you. I don't know what for this is, but [INAUDIBLE]. I don't care much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now, tell about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my brother and my sister. My brother's name is Israel. My sister's name is Freda. They lived in Germany, in Poland, in Warsaw. And they was killed by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year do you think this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941. They was killed in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were killed. When was this photo taken? What year? Do you remember? What year--

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo? This photo was taken maybe two years before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And who was in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife and me. And it was taken-- I don't know, in Germany probably. It was taken in Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would that be?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait one second. And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's me and my wife, taken in 1951. Or, no. Yes. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: What time was the bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: What time? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1961? Yeah. This is Bernie? Manya? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: In back?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's not Abraham? I don't know where is he.

speaker: interviewer
text: This one is-- this is Manya's son?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Teddy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teddy, then Susan, Freda.

speaker: interviewer
text: In front? OK. And in the back, next to Manya, who's standing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Albert.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who is this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then next to Manya--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who it this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to Manya's husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Abraham. It's Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And next to him, who's standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife and me. And this is David-- see, I forgot their name. He used to have a wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan and Freda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where's David? David's on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paula?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's not Paula. It's not Paula.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Paula?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is at the Wailing Wall. OK. What is this, Mr. Zapiler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Israel in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '55? 1975. Yeah, '75.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the wall. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I put it in [INAUDIBLE]-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was with a woman, Helen. My wife was-- she was going without sleeves. And little boys begging. So we gave everybody a couple dollars. Then he run to Helen and spit on her because she [LAUGHS] was going without her sleeves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Without sleeves. Ah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I chased him away. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- describe this one please.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1981. Yeah, 1981. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We make a Seder every year. Seder right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year do you think that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I make Kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I make Kaddish and make a Seder every year, right here. That's David. And on that side is Manya and me and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anniversary?

speaker: interviewee
text: 55th anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, again, say who's in the picture on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's David. That's my wife. And that's me. And that's Manya. And that's the cakes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: One second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is anniversary. My 55th anniversary. Susan--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. On the bottom is Susan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan. She's here. My wife. That's me. And that's my granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nancy.

speaker: interviewee
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nancy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And in back, who've we got?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had a power shortage and the power is back on and we're going to continue to explain who's in the picture, Mr. Zapiler. In the back row, who is on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Susan's-- used to be a husband. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that is my grandson. And that's me. And that's my granddaughter. And that's also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Freda. She's here, right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to Freda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is my grandson, Steve.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to Steve, on the right, in back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teddy is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said, I think, that was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Nancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nancy in front.

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Thank you very much. Mr. Zapiler, we have your family here. Would you all introduce yourselves, please? Who's the little one on your lap? Manya, can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Jason Miklin, great grandson of my father. I am Manya Roland, and this is my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Albert Roland, Manya's husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am David. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm the son of Leo Zapiler.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Elise Zapiler and I'm married to Steven Zapiler, who's the grandson of Leo Zapiler. And we have two children, Aaron and Sarah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Freda Miklin, and I am Mr. Zapiler's granddaughter. And I'm named for his sister, Freda, who raised him. In the picture are two of my five children, Jason and Zachary, who are eight months old. And Alex, Danny, and Becky couldn't be here today but send their love.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Susan Zapiler Grauer. I'm Freda's sister, his daughter, his granddaughter. I have two children. Jessica is 16 and Michael is 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm Ted Roland. I'm Mr. Zapiler's grandson. I'm one half of the Rolands. Bernie Roland couldn't be here today. And I'm Manya Roland and Albert Roland's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do any of you have anything that you'd like to say to your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would only like to acknowledge that it was my father's foresight and his alertness back in 1939 that is directly responsible for our being here today.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to say how proud we are of my grandfather and what a strong and special man he is. And how we've always known that. And also that my husband and I, we say that we're helping to repopulate the world that Hitler tried to eliminate with our five children. And we're happy to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Manya, would you like to say something to your father about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, also thanks to my father's good thinking, we are here. Because he did go to [BACKGROUND NOISE] we survived as a family. And then came to the United States. We lost our mother about 13 years ago. But father's OK. And we're very proud of him. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Susan, did you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing to add.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's got something to say. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to say thanks, Grandpa, because if it wasn't for you, none of us would be here. So thanks a lot. We're very proud of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you all so very much for taking part in this project.
